skill.name: cobrapy
skill.description: Constraint-based reconstruction and analysis (COBRA) for metabolic models; use when you need to simulate growth/production, analyze flux ranges, or run knockout and medium studies from SBML/JSON/YAML models.
skill.summary: Runs constraint-based analysis on metabolic models including FBA, FVA, knockout studies, and medium optimization using the COBRApy Python library.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 156 static analysis findings are false positives. The scanner misidentified markdown code block delimiters as Ruby shell execution patterns, Python import statements as dynamic imports, and documentation URLs as network threats. This is a legitimate bioinformatics documentation skill providing reference workflows for the COBRApy library. The skill is instruction-only with no executable scripts; code examples are for user reference in markdown documentation files.

low_findings.title: Markdown code block misidentified as shell command execution
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The static scanner flagged backtick-delimited Python code blocks in markdown files as Ruby/shell backtick execution. These are standard markdown code fence blocks (```python) used for documentation examples, not executable shell commands. The skill is instruction-only with no automated script execution.

low_findings.title: Python import statements misidentified as dynamic imports
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The scanner flagged standard Python static import statements (from cobra.flux_analysis import ...) as dynamic import() expressions. These are compile-time imports common in all Python code, not runtime dynamic imports that pose security risks.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs misidentified as network threats
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The scanner flagged external URLs as hardcoded network endpoints. These are documentation links to the GitHub repository (SKILL.md:7) and the official COBRApy documentation site (api_quick_reference.md:655). They are reference links, not data exfiltration endpoints.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm detection is a false positive
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The scanner flagged lines containing algorithm mentions or markdown headers as weak cryptographic algorithms. Affected lines contain markdown formatting, API documentation text, and code example strings. No cryptographic implementations exist in this documentation skill.

low_findings.title: Model statistics output misidentified as system reconnaissance
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The scanner flagged print() statements in example code that output model dimensions (reactions, metabolites, genes count) as system reconnaissance. These are standard data exploration steps in scientific computing, not attacker reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: CRITICAL heuristic triggered by pattern combination, no actual threat
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The heuristic analyzer flagged a dangerous combination of code execution + network + credential access patterns. The code execution is Python code examples in markdown, the network is documentation URLs, and no credential access exists. The combined heuristic is a false alarm from legitimate documentation content.

content.value_statement: Metabolic modelers need to simulate growth, analyze flux distributions, and identify essential genes from genome-scale models. This skill provides structured workflows and reference documentation for flux balance analysis, knockout studies, medium optimization, and model validation using the COBRApy library.
actual_capabilities: Run flux balance analysis (FBA) to predict growth and production rates under specified media conditions
Perform flux variability analysis (FVA) to quantify flux uncertainty and identify constrained reactions
Identify essential genes and reactions through systematic single and double knockout analysis
Design and optimize growth media including minimal medium calculation
Run flux sampling to explore feasible solution spaces using OptGP and ACHR methods
Validate and debug metabolic models with mass balance checks and dead-end metabolite analysis
limitations: Requires a compatible SBML, JSON, or YAML model file to perform any analysis
Cannot construct metabolic models from sequence data or literature without pre-built model input
Analysis accuracy depends entirely on the quality and completeness of the input model
Flux sampling and double knockout analysis may be slow on very large genome-scale models

use_cases.title: Metabolic engineering strain design
use_cases.description: Design production strains by identifying beneficial gene knockouts and optimizing flux distributions for target metabolite overproduction.
use_cases.target_user: Metabolic engineers designing microbial production strains

use_cases.title: Essential gene identification
use_cases.description: Run systematic single and double gene deletion screens to classify genes as essential, severely impaired, or neutral for growth.

use_cases.title: Growth medium optimization
use_cases.description: Calculate minimal medium compositions and compare growth requirements across aerobic and anaerobic conditions for target organisms.

prompt_templates.title: Basic FBA simulation
prompt_templates.prompt: Load a COBRApy model from an SBML file and run a standard flux balance analysis simulation. Report the growth rate, optimization status, and key reaction fluxes.

prompt_templates.title: Gene knockout screening
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform single gene deletion analysis on the model. Classify each gene as essential, severely impaired, moderately impaired, or neutral based on growth impact. Report the count for each category and show the top 5 essential genes.
prompt_templates.scenario: Identifying essential genes for a target organism

prompt_templates.title: Flux variability analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Run flux variability analysis on the model at 90% optimal growth. Identify the reactions with the highest flexibility and the reactions that are tightly constrained. Compare with results at full optimal growth.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quantifying flux uncertainty in metabolic networks

prompt_templates.title: Production strain design with knockout optimization
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a production strain for a target metabolite. Calculate the production envelope, identify beneficial single gene knockouts that increase yield, maintain a minimum growth constraint, and test knockout combinations.
prompt_templates.scenario: Optimizing a microbial strain for bioproduction

output_examples.input: Which genes are essential for growth in the E. coli core model?
output_examples.output: Single gene deletion analysis of the E. coli core model classified genes into 67 essential, 210 severely impaired, 345 moderately impaired, and 745 neutral. Essential genes include key metabolic enzymes in glycolysis, TCA cycle, and amino acid biosynthesis pathways.

output_examples.input: What is the minimal medium required for E. coli growth at 50% of maximum?
output_examples.output: The minimal medium for 50% growth requires 4 components: glucose, ammonium, phosphate, and sulfate. Aerobic conditions support this simple composition. Anaerobic conditions require additional electron acceptors and increase the component count.

output_examples.input: Which reactions have the most flux variability in the E. coli core model?
output_examples.output: FVA identified 47 reactions with flexibility above 1.0 mmol/gDW/h at optimal growth. The most flexible include ATP maintenance, glucose transport, and oxygen exchange. At 90% optimality, 23 additional reactions in the TCA cycle and pentose phosphate pathway become flexible.

best_practices: Always validate model feasibility with slim_optimize before running complex multi-step analysis workflows
Use context managers (with model:) for temporary modifications to prevent accidental state carryover across analyses
Set solver timeouts and tolerance values explicitly to ensure reproducible results across different environments
anti_patterns: Modifying exchange reaction bounds directly without using the model.medium dictionary interface for consistent medium state
Running FVA across all reactions without first filtering to a biologically relevant subset to reduce computation time
Interpreting flux values without checking solution status, which can lead to reporting results from infeasible or suboptimal solutions

faq.question: What file formats does COBRApy support?
faq.answer: COBRApy supports SBML, JSON, YAML, and MATLAB model file formats. SBML is the preferred format for interoperability between different modeling tools.

faq.question: Which solvers can I use with COBRApy?
faq.answer: COBRApy supports GLPK (default), CPLEX, and Gurobi solvers. GLPK works for basic FBA. CPLEX or Gurobi is recommended for MILP-based analyses such as minimal medium calculation.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code automatically?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides documentation, reference materials, and workflow templates. Code examples are provided for users to run in their own Python environment.

faq.question: How do I handle an infeasible model?
faq.answer: Check exchange reaction bounds, verify the medium composition, and look for blocked reactions. Use the gapfilling workflow to identify reactions that restore model feasibility.

faq.question: What is the difference between FBA and pFBA?
faq.answer: FBA finds any optimal flux distribution that maximizes the objective. Parsimonious FBA finds the optimal distribution that also minimizes total flux, producing more realistic results by reducing internal cycling.

faq.question: Can I run double knockout analysis on large models?
faq.answer: Yes, but computation time grows quadratically with gene count. Limit the gene list to non-essential genes to reduce the search space and use parallel processing when available.
